narrative:
The market sits comfortably in a business as usual regime, with a stress score of 12 out of 100 confirming that conditions are broadly calm across asset classes. Equity returns are modest and unremarkable, with SPY up 0.9% over five days at a z-score of 0.23, well within normal distributional range, and there is no meaningful signal pointing toward deterioration.

The VIX at 16.6 sits at the 34th percentile of its ten-year history, a level that implies neither complacency nor fear, and the term structure remains in contango, which is the normal, healthy configuration for implied volatility markets. Credit is providing a clear confirmation of the benign backdrop: high yield spreads at 270 basis points sit at just the 7th percentile of their five-year range, and investment grade at 74 basis points carries a z-score of negative 1.20, meaning both markets are historically tight and offering no warning signal whatsoever.

The strongest movers of the past five days are worth noting on the upside, with German equities via EWG gaining 3.7%, UK equities via EWU up 3.3%, and utilities via XLU advancing 3.4%, suggesting some rotation into defensives and European names without any corresponding weakness elsewhere. Not a single sector or emerging market closed in the red over the period, and the weakest mover, FXI at negative 1.9%, represents a modest pullback in Chinese equities rather than a signal of broader stress.
